title: Rust remover paste
subtitle: A citric acid, bicarbonate of soda, and salt paste for rust spots on cast iron, tools, and garden furniture
excerpt: Citric acid dissolves iron oxide (rust) by chelating the iron ions from the surface. Combined with bicarbonate of soda and salt into a paste, it stays in contact with the rust long enough to work. Rinse and dry the metal immediately after use to prevent re-rusting.
sourceNotes: Citric acid rust removal chemistry from standard chemistry references and cast iron restoration guides.

recipe.yieldDescription: Small batch for one cleaning session
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Mix fresh each time. Does not store.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Mix fresh each session. Discard unused paste after 24 hours.

recipeTools.notes: Citric acid can irritate skin with prolonged contact

glossaryTerms.definition: Iron oxide (Fe2O3), formed when iron or steel is exposed to oxygen and moisture. It is a surface compound that weakens the metal over time if not removed.

A chemical process in which an acid (such as citric acid) binds to metal ions and removes them from a surface. Citric acid chelates iron from rust, dissolving the iron oxide without affecting the underlying metal as aggressively as strong mineral acids.
A weak organic acid that removes rust through chelation of iron ions. It is far safer than commercial rust removers based on phosphoric or hydrochloric acid.

The rapid formation of new rust on metal that has been cleaned but left wet. Metal cleaned of rust must be dried thoroughly and immediately and, for cast iron, seasoned with a thin layer of oil to prevent re-rusting.

content.text: binds to the iron ions in the rust and lifts them from the metal surface. Bicarbonate of soda as a paste binder slows the reaction slightly and makes the paste easier to apply and keep in contact with the surface. Dry the metal thoroughly after treatment to prevent
; for cast iron, apply a very thin layer of vegetable oil immediately after drying.

items.prepNote: A few drops to form a thick paste

content.text: Combine citric acid, bicarbonate of soda, and salt in the bowl. Add water a few drops at a time and stir until a thick paste forms. The mixture will fizz slightly as the citric acid reacts with the bicarbonate; this is normal. Apply the paste to rust spots and leave for 30 to 60 minutes, keeping it moist by covering with cling film if needed.

Scrub with a stiff brush. Rinse thoroughly under running water. Dry immediately and completely with a clean cloth. For cast iron, heat briefly in a low oven to ensure all moisture is gone, then apply a very thin coat of vegetable oil while still warm.

Mix fresh for each use. Discard unused paste. The dry ingredients (citric acid, bicarbonate of soda, salt) can be pre-combined in a jar and stored for up to 12 months; add water only at the point of use.
